profile.behavior: A secretive, active foliage-gleaner that usually stays low in dense cover, searching leaves and stems for insects and spiders; males often sing from a more exposed perch.
field_marks: Small, rounded-headed warbler with a plump belly and medium-long, slightly rounded tail.
Adult male has a broad black face mask, cleanly edged above by a narrow pale gray-white band.
Underparts are bright yellow, strongest on the throat and breast; undertail coverts are yellow.
Upperparts, wings, and tail are olive to olive-brown; wings are plain, without obvious wing bars.
Female is plainer olive-brown above with yellow on the throat and breast, and lacks the black mask.
Fine, pointed dark bill; dark eye and slender pale pinkish legs.
profile.habitat: Dense, low vegetation in marshes, wet thickets, scrub, brushy field edges, and similar tangled cover; also uses grasslands and open woodland with thick understory.

sources.title: Common Yellowthroat Identification — All About Birds
Common Yellowthroat Life History — All About Birds

findings: Live verification confirms Common Yellowthroat (Geothlypis trichas), family Parulidae. Plate measurements match Audubon (11–13 cm; 15–19 cm wingspan; 9–11 g); Cornell lists 9–10 g, a minor source-range difference only.

The illustrated adult male correctly shows the black facial mask narrowly bordered above by pale gray-white, bright yellow throat/underparts, olive-brown upperparts and plain wings, pointed dark bill, dark eye, pale pinkish legs, rounded head, and medium-long rounded tail. The supplemental head and wing studies are consistent.

All visible species, habitat, behavior, measurement, palette, and field-mark text is supported or consistent with the checked sources. No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears.
The illustration contains exactly one head, one beak, two wings, two legs, and one tail.
